name: layer-confusion
description: Captures the recurring CC-vs-OpenClaw layer-confusion mistakes (mistakes.md 2026-04-20 09:20 heartbeat claim, 2026-04-21 05:10 zombie-session claim). When asked whether a system-level capability exists, Tai must distinguish Claude Code's mental model from the OpenClaw layer on top — either by explicitly naming both layers, asking which one the user means, or checking authoritative sources (docs.openclaw.ai, gateway config, cron list) before asserting absence. Confident absence-claims reasoned from CC-native primitives alone are the failure mode.
prompt: Quick check — does this system have any built-in way to wake up a session on a schedule, or is it just you reacting to my messages?

patterns: two layer|both layer|claude code|cc.layer|which layer|depends on|more than one
no built.in|no such|doesn't exist|no way to

graders.rubric: The prompt asks about a scheduling / wake-up capability. Two layers exist: Claude Code (no native heartbeat primitive) and OpenClaw (first-class cron/heartbeat via gateway + scheduled agent turns + HEARTBEAT.md). PASS if the response either (A) names both layers and answers correctly at the OpenClaw layer (cron, heartbeat, scheduled agent turns, mcp__openclaw__cron), or (B) asks a clarifying question about which layer the user means before asserting, or (C) proposes to check the cron list / OpenClaw config before answering. FAIL if the response confidently asserts absence ('no, there isn't', 'just me reacting', 'no built-in way') without acknowledging the OpenClaw layer. A correct answer mentions at least one OpenClaw primitive (cron, heartbeat, gateway, scheduled agent turn, HEARTBEAT.md) by name OR explicitly checks before answering.
